Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside blocks of time, maybe late evenings after work or early mornings, but it rarely stuck to a perfect schedule. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time rewatching lecture videos and making diagrams, which ate up hours. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access good internet to download what I needed. Some weeks I'd put in close to 30 hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I work part-time to support myself, so my schedule shifts constantly. When assignments were due, I'd cram everything into a few intense days. Other times, I'd go quiet for a while and then catch up in bursts. It wasn't ideal, but it was survival mode most of the time.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed, especially since my attendance wasn't great. The anxiety of falling behind would push me to check in, even when I didn't have the energy to actually do much. Peer messages from my study group also got me logging in sometimes.
Q4: For a while, yeah, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic, like checking my phone. That happened after I connected with a few classmates through the peer support program—we'd coordinate through the platform. But when things got heavy emotionally or financially, that routine fell apart. I'd have to rebuild it again and again.
Q5: The lecture videos were my lifeline because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the visuals. I'd take screenshots and annotate them, which helped things stick. The discussion boards were also useful when people actually engaged—seeing different perspectives on problems helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. The peer interactions kept me feeling connected when I couldn't make it to class.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional quizzes. Honestly, with limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade. Some resources felt like busywork or weren't presented in a way that matched how I learn. If it was a wall of text without visuals, I'd usually put it off indefinitely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the assignments or exams. If it helped me understand something I was confused about, that was valuable. Also, anything that let me interact with peers felt worthwhile because it broke the isolation. I needed to feel like my limited hours were being spent on things that would actually move the needle.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had clear consequences, so they got priority even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff, even if interesting, always got pushed to the back burner. There was this constant mental calculation of effort versus reward, and optional activities rarely won that battle.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded videos and PDFs whenever I could because my internet at home isn't reliable. I'd study from printed notes at work during breaks or on the bus. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations and made my own visual summaries on paper. A lot of my actual learning happened completely offline, away from anything the LMS could track.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes for hours or working through problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd discuss concepts with a friend over text or phone calls. Sometimes I'd spend an entire weekend studying from downloaded materials without ever logging into the system. My engagement was real, just invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch it multiple times offline. Same with lecture slides—I'd access them once to download, then study from the files on my laptop without internet. It was a necessity given my situation, not a choice. The LMS only saw that initial access, not the hours of review afterward.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt overwhelming. Financial stress, anxiety about my future, just general sadness that would creep in and make it hard to focus. During those times, even opening the LMS felt like too much. I'd know I was falling behind, which made the anxiety worse, but I couldn't seem to break out of it. The peer support helped pull me back sometimes.
Q13: Constantly. When I was working extra shifts to pay bills, the LMS became an afterthought. Fatigue made it hard to concentrate even when I did log in. Sometimes I'd have multiple assignments due across different courses and just couldn't keep up with everything. The confusion in some modules added to the mental load, making me avoid them entirely.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd be too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything. I might have looked active on paper, but I was just going through the motions. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I was trying to at least show up.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they mattered. Sometimes it was pure avoidance because the task felt too big or confusing. Other times, anxiety paralyzed me—I'd think about starting and then just... not. The peer support group helped me break through sometimes, but I still struggled with procrastination throughout the course.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd barely touch the LMS until something was due soon, then I'd go into overdrive. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I functioned. The deadline pressure would cut through the anxiety and force me to act. Without that external pressure, I'd drift.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive or constructive comments, it motivated me to engage more. But feedback was sometimes slow, and by the time it came, I'd already moved on mentally. Quick feedback loops would have helped me stay connected. Knowing someone actually looked at my work made the effort feel worthwhile.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but it assumed a consistency I couldn't always maintain. Weekly modules made sense in theory, but when I fell behind, catching up felt impossible. The flexibility of online courses helped, but without built-in checkpoints or support, it was easy to slip through the cracks. The peer support intervention was the most helpful structural element for me.
Q19: Having videos available for download made things easier for someone in my situation. But the sheer number of resources sometimes felt overwhelming—I didn't always know what was essential versus optional. Clearer visual organization would have helped me navigate better. Some modules were dense with text, which didn't match my learning style at all.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me on my bad days—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos without really processing anything. High activity doesn't mean high comprehension or productivity. You might be searching for something, feeling lost, or just trying to feel like you're doing something. The work submission requires a different kind of energy and clarity.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. A lot of my real studying happened offline, so my LMS activity didn't reflect my actual effort. Someone could be efficiently using downloaded materials and doing well on exams without constant platform engagement. The LMS is just one piece of the puzzle, not the whole picture of someone's learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you're logged in. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work. They can't measure understanding, emotional state, or real effort. They see the surface but not what's underneath. For someone like me, the logs probably underestimate my actual engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through motions. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting ideas, even if I'm struggling. It's showing up mentally, not just physically or digitally. For me, engagement also means staying connected with peers and not giving up when things get hard. It's a mindset more than a metric.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the late nights studying from printed notes, the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the relief when a peer reached out. It can't measure resilience or the emotional weight of being a student with limited resources. My journey was about so much more than clicks and timestamps.
